title: Commercial Roofing in Southbridge, MA | Commercial Roofers of Worcester

html:
Sawtooth Roofs and Subdivided Industrial Space in Southbridge

Southbridge grew up around American Optical's manufacturing campus, and a lot of the town's commercial roof stock still reflects that history: long low-slope buildings, sawtooth monitor roofs built for daylight before electric lighting was reliable, and large floor plates that have since been carved up among several smaller tenants. We work these buildings section by section rather than treating the whole roof as one uniform system.

Sawtooth and Monitor Roof Sections

Older manufacturing buildings in Southbridge often still have sawtooth or monitor roof profiles, vertical glazed sections that once let daylight into the shop floor below. These sections are usually the weakest part of the roof today: the glazing gaskets dry out, the flashing at the base of each monitor is a long run of detail work rather than a simple field seam, and water finds its way in at the transition between the sloped glazing wall and the flat roof deck.

When we quote a reroof on one of these buildings, the sawtooth sections get priced separately from the flat field membrane, because the labor per square foot on that detail work is several times what it costs to install membrane on open flat roof.

Some owners choose to remove non-functional monitor sections entirely and rebuild that portion of the roof as standard flat low-slope membrane, which can lower long-term maintenance cost significantly, though it changes the building's interior daylighting and sometimes requires a structural review of what the monitor framing was originally supporting.

Subdivided Buildings and Mismatched Roof Ages

As the old optical campus and similar industrial buildings were divided among smaller manufacturers and storage tenants, roof work over the decades tended to happen tenant by tenant rather than as one coordinated project. The result is roofs with several distinct sections of different ages and even different membrane types butted against each other, sometimes with nothing more than a bead of mastic holding the transition together.

Our first step on these buildings is always mapping where those transitions actually are, since a leak reported in one tenant's space frequently traces back to a seam failure in a completely different section of roof.

A few conditions we check on every subdivided industrial building:

  • Transition seams between different membrane generations
  • Sawtooth or monitor glazing base flashing
  • Roof drains serving multiple tenant spaces below
  • Structural steel condition at older skylight curbs
  • Rooftop equipment added independently by separate tenants
  • Parapet and expansion joint continuity across sections

Small Industry and Route 131 Commercial Buildings

Beyond the old campus, Southbridge's smaller manufacturers and service businesses along Route 131 and the town's commercial corridors tend to occupy simpler steel-frame buildings with a single low-slope roof and a more straightforward maintenance history. These are usually good candidates for a single mechanically attached membrane system, since they don't carry the layered history of the older industrial buildings.

Harrington HealthCare's presence in town also means we get calls on smaller medical office buildings, where roof-top condensing units for imaging or refrigeration equipment need flashing details built to a higher standard than a typical retail curb.

Several of these smaller commercial and medical office buildings sit along streets that once served the optical campus's overflow parking and support functions, and their roofs were often built to a lighter commercial standard than the industrial buildings nearby. We check deck load capacity carefully whenever a tenant wants to add rooftop equipment on one of these lighter-built structures.

Pricing a Southbridge Roof by Section

Because so much of Southbridge's commercial stock has mixed roof ages, we price by section rather than by a single blended rate. A 60-mil TPO field membrane on open flat roof runs a very different number than the labor-heavy work of re-flashing a sawtooth base or matching a new membrane into a 1990s-era EPDM section that's staying in place for now.

On any project where we're only replacing part of the roof, we spec transition detailing to manufacturer standard so the new section carries its warranty independently, rather than letting the existing roof's condition void coverage on the work we just completed.

Questions Southbridge Owners Ask Us

What makes sawtooth roof sections more expensive to maintain?

The glazing and flashing detail at the base of each monitor is labor-intensive compared to flat field membrane, and gasket or flashing failure there is one of the most common leak sources in older Southbridge industrial buildings.

Can you replace just one section of a subdivided industrial roof?

Yes, and it's common here. We detail the transition to the remaining roof to manufacturer spec so the new section's warranty isn't compromised by adjacent older roofing.

How do you handle roof leaks that show up in one tenant's space but originate elsewhere?

We trace water paths across the roof rather than assuming the leak is directly above where it's showing inside, which is especially important on buildings with several tenants and mismatched roof ages.

Are older industrial roofs in Southbridge usually recoverable or do they need full tear-off?

It depends on the section. We run moisture scans on each roof area independently since one part of a subdivided building can be dry while another has saturated insulation underneath.

Do medical office buildings need different rooftop equipment flashing?

Yes. Imaging and refrigeration condensing units are heavier and run more continuously than typical HVAC, so we build curb flashing to handle that load and vibration over time.
